1:
On Galatians 2:1 — “Then fourteen years after I went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, and took Titus with me also” — the witnesses mark the second Jerusalem visit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the date and company. Robertson: “after the space of fourteen years… points of contact with the apostles.” Ellicott reckons the fourteen years from the previous visit. Calvin doubts it is the journey of Acts 15:2; Wesley and others identify it with the Jerusalem council. The Lutheran Luther stresses the long interval of independence.

The verse opens the account of a later visit. After fourteen years Paul went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, taking Titus. The long gap underscores his independence; the companions set the stage for the test case.

So the witnesses see Paul resume his narrative with a second, much later visit to Jerusalem. The witnesses note the long interval — “fourteen years after” — reckoned by most from the first visit, marking how many years Paul had laboured independently before this further contact with the apostles; the gap itself proves he was no dependent of theirs. They mark the companions: Barnabas, his fellow-worker among the Gentiles, and Titus, an uncircumcised Greek convert — brought along, as the next verses show, as a living test case of the gospel of grace. They observe the debate among interpreters over which visit this was: many identify it with the Jerusalem council of Acts 15, where the question of Gentile circumcision was settled, though Calvin and some demur, placing it earlier. They note Luther’s emphasis on the long years of independence the date implies. The verse thus begins the second movement of the autobiographical defence: not now Paul’s avoidance of the apostles, but his eventual meeting with them on equal terms, when the gospel of grace was vindicated and Titus left uncircumcised. The bringing of Titus signals the issue about to be joined.
2:
On Galatians 2:2 — “And I went up by revelation, and communicated unto them that gospel which I preach among the Gentiles, but privately to them which were of reputation, lest by any means I should run, or had run, in vain” — the witnesses mark the divine prompting and the private conference.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the harmony with Acts. Robertson: “by revelation” (kata apokalypsin) — though Acts 15:2 says the church sent them, “surely there is no inconsistency.” Wesley: he laid the gospel before them “not that they might confirm me… but that” the truth might be cleared. The Reformed Calvin: God directed the journey for the confirmation of his doctrine.

The verse explains the visit’s prompting and manner. Paul went by revelation and laid his gospel before the leaders privately, lest his work be in vain. The conference was God-directed and discreet.

So the witnesses see Paul go up at God’s prompting and confer with the leaders, not to be corrected but to safeguard the gospel’s course. The witnesses note that he went “by revelation” — by divine direction — which, they observe, harmonizes with Acts’ record that the church also sent him; the human and divine impulses agree. They mark that he “communicated” the gospel he preached among the Gentiles, laying it before the apostles — not to receive it or have it confirmed, for he was sure of it, but to demonstrate its agreement with theirs and so secure the unity of the church. They observe the discretion: “privately to them which were of reputation” — a private conference with the leading apostles, to avoid needless controversy before the whole assembly. They note the aim: “lest… I should run, or had run, in vain” — not doubt of his gospel, but concern that division between him and the Jerusalem leaders might frustrate his labours. The verse thus shows Paul, though independent, seeking the open concord of the apostles for the gospel’s sake: divinely sent, he laid his message before them privately, that its truth might stand and his work not be hindered by a breach.
3:
On Galatians 2:3 — “But neither Titus, who was with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised” — the witnesses mark the test case of Titus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the decisive instance. Barnes: Paul introduces Titus “to show that circumcision was not necessary to salvation” — a case “just in point.” Robertson notes the curious debate over whether he was circumcised, but the text says “not compelled.” The Reformed Calvin: the apostles did not hesitate to acknowledge an uncircumcised man as a brother. The Lutheran Luther: this shows the conference’s conclusion.

The verse gives the proof in practice. Titus, a Greek, was not compelled to be circumcised. The apostles’ refusal to require it vindicated the gospel of grace for the Gentiles.

So the witnesses see in Titus the living vindication of Paul’s gospel. The witnesses note that Titus was a Greek, an uncircumcised Gentile convert, deliberately brought by Paul as a test case: if circumcision were necessary to salvation, the apostles would have required it of him. They mark the decisive outcome: he “was not compelled to be circumcised” — the Jerusalem leaders did not insist on it, but received him as a brother in Christ uncircumcised. They observe Calvin’s point that the apostles’ acceptance of an uncircumcised man as a fellow-believer settled the principle: circumcision is not required for justification or church membership. They note Luther’s observation that this very fact reveals what the conference concluded — that the Gentiles need not be circumcised. The verse thus furnishes the practical proof of the gospel of grace: in the very presence of the Jerusalem apostles, an uncircumcised Gentile was owned as a Christian, demonstrating that the Judaizers’ demand had no apostolic warrant. Titus uncircumcised is the standing refutation of the false gospel — though, as the next verse shows, the matter was contested by false brethren.
4:
On Galatians 2:4 — “And that because of false brethren unawares brought in, who came in privily to spy out our liberty which we have in Christ Jesus, that they might bring us into bondage” — the witnesses mark the false brethren.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the intruders. Robertson: “false brethren privately brought in” (pareisaktous pseudadelphous), brought in on the sly. Barnes: Judaizing Christians, claiming the faith but binding the law. Wesley: they “slipped in to spy out our liberty… that they might bring us into bondage.” The Reformed Calvin: they would make Titus’s circumcision an occasion of slander.

The verse exposes the source of the pressure: false brethren. Counterfeit believers, smuggled in, came to spy out Christian liberty and reduce it to bondage. They lay behind the demand to circumcise Titus.

So the witnesses see Paul unmask the false brethren whose intrusion made Titus a contested case. The witnesses note their character: “false brethren” — men professing Christ but at heart enemies of the gospel of grace — “unawares brought in,” smuggled into the church and its councils by stealth, as spies are slipped into a camp. They mark their malicious aim: “to spy out our liberty which we have in Christ Jesus” — the freedom from the yoke of the ceremonial law — “that they might bring us into bondage,” to enslave believers again under the law. They observe that these are the men, not the apostles, who pressed for Titus’s circumcision; the leaders’ refusal was a stand against them. They note Calvin’s point that the false brethren watched for any concession to turn into a precedent and a slander. The verse thus identifies the real enemy: not the Jerusalem apostles, who agreed with Paul, but covert false brethren bent on destroying gospel liberty. Their stealthy assault explains why the matter of Titus was a battle — and why Paul, as the next verse declares, yielded to them not for a moment.
5:
On Galatians 2:5 — “To whom we gave place by subjection, no, not for an hour; that the truth of the gospel might continue with you” — the witnesses mark the unyielding stand.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the firmness. Robertson: “no, not for an hour” (oude pros horan) — pointed denial that they yielded “in the way of subjection.” Barnes: “we did not submit to this at all… not… for the shortest time.” Wesley: “circumcising Timothy… because of weak brethren, but not Titus, because of false brethren.” The Reformed Calvin: this steadiness “was the seal of Paul’s doctrine.”

The verse records Paul’s refusal to yield. He did not submit to the false brethren even for an hour, that the truth of the gospel might be preserved for the Galatians. The stand was firm and principled.

So the witnesses see Paul stand immovable against the false brethren, refusing the least concession. The witnesses note the emphatic firmness: “no, not for an hour” — not for the briefest moment did he and his companions submit to the demand to circumcise Titus; they gave no ground at all in the way of subjection. They mark the discernment Wesley draws out: Paul was not rigid for its own sake — he circumcised Timothy for the sake of weak brethren, in a matter of indifference — but he would not circumcise Titus, because here it was demanded by false brethren as necessary to salvation, which would betray the gospel. They observe the principle at stake: “that the truth of the gospel might continue with you” — had he yielded, the doctrine of free justification would have been compromised, and the Galatians robbed of it. They note Calvin’s point that this steadfastness sealed Paul’s doctrine, removing all doubt. The verse thus shows the line firmly drawn: in things indifferent, charity may yield; but where the truth of the gospel is at stake, no concession is lawful. Paul’s refusal preserved gospel liberty for the Galatians and for the church.
6:
On Galatians 2:6 — “But of these who seemed to be somewhat… God accepteth no man’s person: for they who seemed to be somewhat in conference added nothing to me” — the witnesses mark his equality with the chief apostles.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the phrase. Robertson: “somewhat” (ti), the Big Three (Cephas, James, John) — Paul asserts his independence “sharply.” Adam Clarke: “those who were of acknowledged reputation,” not a slight. Wesley: “God accepteth no man’s person… in that conference added nothing to me.” The Lutheran Luther: he abases their dignity to magnify the truth.

The verse asserts Paul’s parity with the leading apostles. From those of reputation he received nothing new; God shows no partiality to persons. The chief apostles added nothing to his gospel.

So the witnesses see Paul affirm his full equality with the most eminent apostles. The witnesses note the careful phrase — “who seemed to be somewhat,” or were of acknowledged reputation — by which Paul refers, without disrespect, to the leading apostles, Peter, James, and John, while asserting his own independence. They mark the principle he interjects: “God accepteth no man’s person” — God shows no partiality to rank or reputation; the eminence of the apostles does not make their gospel truer or Paul’s inferior. They observe the decisive fact: “in conference added nothing to me” — the chief apostles, hearing his gospel, found nothing to correct or supplement; they added no doctrine, imposed no condition, but recognized his message as complete and true. They note Luther’s point that Paul abases their titles only to exalt the truth above all persons. The verse thus clinches the argument of independence: the very pillars of the church, on hearing Paul’s gospel, contributed nothing to it, confirming that it was already full and divine. His message stood in need of no human ratification, even from the greatest — for they themselves owned it whole.
7:
On Galatians 2:7 — “But contrariwise, when they saw that the gospel of the uncircumcision was committed unto me, as the gospel of the circumcision was unto Peter” — the witnesses mark the recognized division of labour.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel. Robertson: “but on the contrary” — the three came “boldly to Paul’s side.” Ellicott: they recognized his teaching as “fundamentally the same as their own,” his success proving the divine call. Adam Clarke: his doctrine came “immediately from the same source.” The Gentile mission was committed to Paul as the Jewish to Peter.

The verse states the apostles’ positive recognition. Far from correcting Paul, they saw that the gospel for the Gentiles was entrusted to him as that for the Jews was to Peter. They acknowledged his distinct, God-given commission.

So the witnesses see the Jerusalem leaders not merely refrain from correcting Paul but positively recognize his commission. The witnesses note the contrast: “but contrariwise” — so far from siding with the Judaizers or finding fault, the apostles saw and acknowledged that God had entrusted to Paul “the gospel of the uncircumcision,” the mission to the Gentiles, just as he had given Peter “the gospel of the circumcision,” the mission to the Jews. They mark that this is one gospel with two spheres of labour, not two gospels: the same message, divinely apportioned to different fields. They observe Ellicott’s point that the apostles recognized Paul’s teaching as fundamentally identical to their own, and his success among the Gentiles as proof of his divine call. They note that this recognition by the pillars themselves utterly demolishes the Judaizers’ claim that Paul was an inferior or deviant apostle. The verse thus turns the apostles into witnesses for Paul: they saw his Gentile commission as God’s own appointment, parallel to Peter’s — a recognition grounded, as the next verse says, in the evident working of God through both.
8:
On Galatians 2:8 — “For he that wrought effectually in Peter to the apostleship of the circumcision, the same was mighty in me toward the Gentiles” — the witnesses mark the divine working in both.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the same word. Ellicott: “wrought effectually” and “was mighty” are the same Greek verb (energeo) — the same divine energy in both. Robertson: Paul recognizes Peter’s leadership to the Jews and asserts his own to the Gentiles. Adam Clarke: “he gave us both those talents… and equal success.” The Reformed Calvin: the divine power was the seal attesting his office.

The verse grounds the parallel in God’s working. The God who worked powerfully through Peter for the Jews worked the same in Paul for the Gentiles. The same divine energy attested both apostleships.

So the witnesses see the evidence on which the apostles recognized Paul: the same God working mightily through him as through Peter. The witnesses note that the verb is identical in both clauses — God “wrought effectually” in Peter and “was mighty” in Paul, one and the same divine energy operating through each. They mark that this working is the proof of a true apostleship: the conversions, the signs, the manifest power of God accompanying the ministry, were as evident in Paul’s Gentile mission as in Peter’s Jewish one. They observe Calvin’s point that such divine energy is the seal authenticating the office and the doctrine; God would not so bless a false apostle or a corrupt gospel. They note that the apostles, seeing this seal upon Paul’s labours, could not but acknowledge his commission as God’s own. The verse thus supplies the ground of the recognition in verse 7: the same God who validated Peter validated Paul, by the same powerful working. Paul’s apostleship to the Gentiles stands on the identical footing as Peter’s to the Jews — divinely commissioned and divinely attested — which moved the pillars to extend the hand of fellowship.
9:
On Galatians 2:9 — “And when James, Cephas, and John, who seemed to be pillars, perceived the grace that was given unto me, they gave to me and Barnabas the right hands of fellowship” — the witnesses mark the right hand of fellowship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the pillars. Robertson: “pillars” (styloi), columns, the “Pillar Apostles” (1 Timothy 3:15). Wesley: James named first as “bishop of… Jerusalem.” The Reformed Calvin: they who despise the grace which the apostles revered are charged with proud disdain. The Lutheran Luther: Peter gave testimony that Paul was a true apostle.

The verse records the formal recognition. The pillar apostles, perceiving the grace given to Paul, gave him and Barnabas the right hand of fellowship. The leaders publicly owned him as a true apostle.

So the witnesses see the pillar apostles formally and gladly acknowledge Paul. The witnesses note their standing: James, Cephas (Peter), and John, “who seemed to be pillars” — the chief supports of the church, men of the highest authority. They mark what moved them: they “perceived the grace that was given unto me” — recognizing the divine grace evident in Paul’s call, gifts, and fruitful labours, the same grace that had made the persecutor an apostle. They observe the gesture: “the right hands of fellowship” — the solemn token of partnership, agreement, and mutual recognition, extended to Paul and Barnabas as fellow-labourers and equals. They note Luther’s point that by this the very pillars testified that Paul was a true apostle, and Calvin’s that those who now disdained him scorned what the apostles themselves revered. The verse thus brings the proof of independence and equality to its climax: not corrected, not subordinated, but openly received into full fellowship by the greatest of the apostles, who saw God’s grace in him. Paul’s gospel and commission are vindicated by the pillars’ own hands — with only one request attached, which the next verse names.
10:
On Galatians 2:10 — “Only they would that we should remember the poor; the same which I also was forward to do” — the witnesses mark the one request.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the single charge. Robertson: “that we should keep on remembering” the poor; Paul had already done so. Ellicott: the poor at Jerusalem and Judea (Acts 11:29–30). Adam Clarke: they added no new injunction, only this. The Lutheran Luther: the office of a faithful pastor is to be mindful of the poor.

The verse names the sole condition attached to the fellowship. The apostles asked only that Paul and Barnabas remember the poor — which Paul was already eager to do. No doctrinal addition, only a charge of love.

So the witnesses see that the pillars added to their fellowship but one request, and that not doctrinal. The witnesses note the significance of “only”: the apostles imposed no new teaching, no condition of circumcision or law-keeping, nothing touching the gospel — the single thing they asked was “that we should remember the poor.” They mark that this refers to the poor believers of Jerusalem and Judea, impoverished by persecution and famine, for whose relief Paul did indeed labour to gather contributions from the Gentile churches. They observe that this confirms the whole argument: had the apostles thought Paul’s gospel defective, they would have added doctrine; instead they added only a work of charity, which proves they found his message complete. They note that Paul was “forward” — already eager — to do this, so the request laid no burden on him. They observe Luther’s point that remembrance of the poor belongs to faithful pastoral care. The verse thus seals the conference: full doctrinal agreement, full fellowship, and only one loving request — the care of the poor — which Paul gladly embraced. The unity of the apostles around the one gospel is complete.
11:
On Galatians 2:11 — “But when Peter was come to Antioch, I withstood him to the face, because he was to be blamed” — the witnesses mark the confrontation at Antioch.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the boldness. Robertson: “I resisted him to the face… I stood against him face to face” — Paul faced Peter as his equal. Adam Clarke records the old controversy whether this was Peter the apostle (most say yes). The Reformed Calvin places it before the Acts 15 decree. The Lutheran Luther: Paul not only had Peter’s testimony but withstood him publicly.

The verse records a sharp public rebuke. When Peter came to Antioch, Paul opposed him to his face, for he was in the wrong. Even the chief apostle was not above correction.

So the witnesses see Paul give the crowning proof of his equality: he openly rebuked Peter himself. The witnesses note the boldness — “I withstood him to the face” — not behind his back nor with deference, but face to face, as an equal confronting an equal, when the truth of the gospel was at stake. They mark the ground: “because he was to be blamed” — Peter was genuinely in the wrong, and Paul does not soften it. They observe the old question, raised by Clarke, whether this “Peter” (or Cephas) was the apostle or another; the great majority hold it was Peter the apostle, which is the whole point of the example. They note Calvin’s view that this occurred before the Jerusalem decree settled the matter. They observe that this incident clinches Paul’s argument: so far from being inferior to or dependent on the twelve, he could and did correct the foremost of them when he erred. The verse thus turns the confrontation into the final proof of his apostolic standing — and, more importantly, into a defence of the gospel itself, for Peter’s fault, as the next verses show, struck at the truth of justification by faith.
12:
On Galatians 2:12 — “For before that certain came from James, he did eat with the Gentiles: but when they were come, he withdrew and separated himself, fearing them which were of the circumcision” — the witnesses mark Peter’s inconsistency.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the change. Robertson: “before… certain came from James” — the reason for Paul’s condemnation. Adam Clarke: “here was Peter’s fault” — convinced the wall was down, he ate with Gentiles, then drew back. The Reformed Calvin: Peter withdrew “for the sake of the Jews,” to avoid offending the circumcision party.

The verse describes Peter’s fault. He had freely eaten with the Gentile believers; but when men came from James, he drew back and separated, fearing the circumcision party. His conduct contradicted his conviction.

So the witnesses see the specific fault for which Paul rebuked Peter. The witnesses note that Peter, knowing the ceremonial wall between Jew and Gentile was abolished, had freely “eaten with the Gentiles” at Antioch — sharing table-fellowship in defiance of the old food-laws, as his vision at Joppa had taught him. They mark the reversal: “when they were come” — certain men from James, of the circumcision party — he “withdrew and separated himself,” ceasing to eat with the Gentiles. They observe the motive: “fearing them which were of the circumcision” — not from conviction (he knew better) but from fear of the Jewish party’s disapproval. They note that this was not heresy but inconsistency — Peter acting against his own knowledge, out of human respect. They observe Calvin’s point that he withdrew for the sake of the Jews, to avoid giving offence, but in so doing gave a far graver offence. The verse thus lays bare the fault: Peter’s timid withdrawal, though not a denial of doctrine in words, betrayed the gospel in practice, implying that the Gentiles must be kept at arm’s length unless they Judaized — the very error Paul could not tolerate.
13:
On Galatians 2:13 — “And the other Jews dissembled likewise with him; insomuch that Barnabas also was carried away with their dissimulation” — the witnesses mark the spreading hypocrisy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contagion. Robertson: “dissembled likewise” (synypekrithesan), to join in pretending. Charles Ellicott: the “hypocrisy” was “suppressing their real convictions.” Wesley: even Barnabas “was borne away, as with a torrent.” The Lutheran Luther: Peter’s dissimulation, known by him to be false, drew the rest after.

The verse shows the fault spreading. The other Jewish believers joined Peter’s pretence, and even Barnabas was swept along. One leader’s inconsistency carried away many.

So the witnesses see how Peter’s example infected the whole company. The witnesses note the spread: “the other Jews dissembled likewise” — the Jewish believers at Antioch, following Peter’s lead, also withdrew from table-fellowship with the Gentiles, acting against their own convictions. They mark the word “dissembled” or “hypocrisy” — the literal sense being to act a part, to suppress one’s true belief and put on a false show; they knew the gospel had freed them, yet pretended otherwise out of fear. They observe the grievous extent: “even Barnabas” — Paul’s trusted fellow-labourer among the Gentiles, the very man who had championed their cause — “was carried away,” swept along as by a torrent into the same dissimulation. They note the lesson on the power of a leader’s bad example: when Peter, the foremost, faltered, the contagion ran swiftly through the rest. The verse thus shows why Paul had to act: the fault was not Peter’s alone but, through him, had drawn away the whole Jewish-Christian body and even Barnabas, threatening to divide the church and betray the gospel in practice. A public wrong demanded a public rebuke — which Paul now delivers.
14:
On Galatians 2:14 — “But when I saw that they walked not uprightly according to the truth of the gospel, I said unto Peter before them all, If thou, being a Jew, livest after the manner of Gentiles… why compellest thou the Gentiles to live as do the Jews?” — the witnesses mark the public rebuke.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the charge. Robertson: “walked not uprightly” (orthopodousin, walk straight) — in time Paul saw and spoke. Adam Clarke: “according to that true doctrine” that Christ is the end of the law. Wesley: “Paul single against Peter and all the Jews.” The Reformed Calvin: the way to truth was to unite Jew and Gentile.

The verse gives the open confrontation and its logic. Seeing they walked crookedly, Paul publicly challenged Peter: if you, a Jew, live like a Gentile, why force Gentiles to live like Jews? The inconsistency is exposed before all.

So the witnesses see Paul rebuke Peter openly and unanswerably. The witnesses note that he acted on what he saw — that they “walked not uprightly,” did not walk straight “according to the truth of the gospel” — their conduct swerving from the doctrine of free grace and Jew-Gentile unity in Christ. They mark the publicity: “before them all” — the open fault required an open rebuke, that the harm done in public might be undone in public. They observe the unanswerable logic of the charge: Peter, a Jew, had himself been living “after the manner of Gentiles,” freely eating with them, abandoning the food-laws; how then could he, by his withdrawal, “compel the Gentiles to live as do the Jews” — pressuring them to adopt Jewish customs to retain fellowship? His own former practice condemned his present conduct. They note that Paul stood alone against Peter and the whole drifting company, fearless for the gospel. The verse thus delivers the rebuke and its argument: Peter’s withdrawal, by implying that Gentiles must Judaize, contradicted both his own conduct and the truth of the gospel — and so opens onto the great doctrinal statement of justification by faith that follows.
15:
On Galatians 2:15 — “We who are Jews by nature, and not sinners of the Gentiles” — the witnesses mark the transition to doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the turn. Robertson: it is unclear whether vv. 15–21 were spoken to Peter or now addressed to the Galatians. Adam Clarke: “sinners of the Gentiles” — the Jewish term for the heathen, without the law. Wesley: “Jews by nature — by birth, not proselytes only.” The Puritan Henry: Paul now speaks of “the great foundation doctrine of the gospel.”

The verse begins the doctrinal argument. We are Jews by birth, not Gentile sinners. Even those with the advantage of the law must look elsewhere for justification.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the rebuke of Peter into the great doctrine of justification. The witnesses note that whether these words were spoken to Peter at Antioch or now addressed to the Galatians, they carry the argument from the particular fault to the universal principle. They mark the starting point, stated with a touch of irony from the Jewish viewpoint: “we who are Jews by nature” — born within the covenant, possessing the law — “and not sinners of the Gentiles,” the term Jews used for the heathen who lacked the law and lived outside it. They observe that Paul takes up the very advantage on which the Judaizers leaned — Jewish birth and the law — in order to show that even this gives no ground of justification. They note Henry’s point that Paul here lays the foundation doctrine of the gospel. The verse thus sets the stage for the central truth of the epistle: if even those born Jews, with all the privileges of the law, are not justified by the law, then assuredly the Gentiles are not — and all alike must be justified by faith in Christ. The argument from Peter’s inconsistency opens into the doctrine that overturns the whole Judaizing position.
16:
On Galatians 2:16 — “Knowing that a man is not justified by the works of the law, but by the faith of Jesus Christ… for by the works of the law shall no flesh be justified” — the witnesses mark the central doctrine of justification.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the great word. Robertson: “is not justified” (dikaioutai), to declare righteous, one of the great Pauline words. Adam Clarke points to Romans 3:20; Romans 3:28. The Reformed Calvin: “not by works, but by the gift of Christ alone.” The Lutheran Luther: “the work of the law” means all that is contrary to grace. Wesley: not even the moral, much less the ceremonial, law.

The verse states the heart of the gospel. A man is not justified by works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ; by works of the law no one is justified. Righteousness before God is by faith alone.

So the witnesses see Paul declare the cardinal doctrine of the epistle and of the Reformation: justification by faith, not works. The witnesses note the meaning of “justified” — to be declared righteous, acquitted, accepted before God’s tribunal; and the emphatic exclusion: “not by the works of the law,” not by any obedience, ceremonial or moral, that man can render. They mark the positive ground: “but by the faith of Jesus Christ” — by trusting in Christ and his finished work, which alone avails. They observe the universality of the negative, thrice stated: “by the works of the law shall no flesh be justified” — no human being, Jew or Gentile, can be accepted on the footing of law-keeping, for all have sinned. They note Luther’s sweeping definition that “the works of the law” embrace everything that is not grace, and Calvin’s that justification is by the gift of Christ alone. They observe that this single truth overturns the whole Judaizing system and grounds Paul’s rebuke of Peter. The verse thus states the doctrine on which the gospel stands or falls: the sinner is justified before God not by his own works but by faith in Christ — the truth Peter’s conduct had obscured and Paul here vindicates.
17:
On Galatians 2:17 — “But if, while we seek to be justified by Christ, we ourselves also are found sinners, is therefore Christ the minister of sin? God forbid” — the witnesses mark the answered objection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the difficulty. Robertson: Jews who thought themselves not sinners, brought close to Christ, “found that they were”; “a minister of sin” — objective genitive. Charles Ellicott frames the objection: if seeking justification in Christ left us “sinners on a par with the Gentiles,” was something more needed? Calvin returns it to the Galatians.

The verse meets an objection. If, seeking justification in Christ, we are found to be sinners like the Gentiles, does that make Christ a promoter of sin? Far from it. Paul indignantly denies the inference.

So the witnesses see Paul rebut a charge brought against the gospel of grace. The witnesses note the objection, somewhat compressed: if Jews, abandoning the law to seek justification in Christ, thereby put themselves on the level of “sinners of the Gentiles” — outside the law — does it follow that Christ, in leading them to forsake the law, is “the minister of sin,” a promoter of sinful living? They mark Paul’s vehement denial: “God forbid” — the thought is abhorrent. They observe the deeper truth in the objection itself: those who come to Christ for justification do indeed discover that they are sinners, for the gospel strips away every pretension of self-righteousness; but this is no fault of Christ’s — it is the necessary first step to grace. They note the Judaizing logic Paul refutes: that to leave the law for faith opens the door to sin and makes Christ its servant. The verse thus defends the gospel against the slander that justification by faith promotes lawlessness: to seek righteousness in Christ alone, far from making him a minister of sin, is the only way to true righteousness — a point Paul will clinch by showing that returning to the law, not leaving it, is the real transgression.
18:
On Galatians 2:18 — “For if I build again the things which I destroyed, I make myself a transgressor” — the witnesses mark where the real fault lies.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the turn. Robertson: “a transgressor” (parabaten) — Peter by his shifts contradicted himself. Adam Clarke: if I enjoin the law I once abolished, “I build up what I destroyed.” Charles Ellicott: the sin is “in going back from Christ to the Law.” The Reformed Calvin: the supposition is at variance with his whole doctrine.

The verse locates the transgression. If I rebuild what I tore down — the law as a way of justification — I prove myself a transgressor. The fault is in returning to the law, not in leaving it.

So the witnesses see Paul turn the charge back upon his opponents: the real transgressor is the one who returns to the law. The witnesses note the figure of building and destroying: having “destroyed” the law as a ground of justification — preached its insufficiency and pointed men to Christ alone — if he were now to “build again” that same fallen structure, re-imposing the law as necessary to acceptance, he would convict himself as a “transgressor.” They mark the application to Peter: by withdrawing from the Gentiles, Peter was rebuilding the very wall he had helped tear down, and so condemning his own former, right action. They observe Ellicott’s point that the sin lies not in leaving the law for Christ but in going back from Christ to the law. They note that this neatly answers verse 17: it is not seeking justification in Christ that makes one a sinner, but reverting to the law. The verse thus reverses the Judaizers’ accusation: the transgression is theirs, for they would rebuild the abolished system. To forsake Christ for the law is the real fault — and Paul will now show, from his own experience, how thoroughly the believer has died to the law to live to God.
19:
On Galatians 2:19 — “For I through the law am dead to the law, that I might live unto God” — the witnesses mark death to the law for life to God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the paradox. Robertson: “I through the law died to the law” — paradoxical but true (Romans 7:4). Adam Clarke: considering the law’s requirements, he was “obliged to take refuge in the Gospel.” The Reformed Calvin: the law itself slays its disciples; Christ need not destroy its righteousness. Wesley: dead to the law “to all hope of justification from it… that I may live to God.”

The verse states the believer’s death to the law. Through the law itself Paul died to the law, that he might live to God. The law, doing its work, drove him from itself to Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul explain how the believer is freed from the law for a life toward God. The witnesses note the paradox: “through the law am dead to the law” — the law itself, by its demands and its curse, slew every hope of justification by works, convicting Paul of sin and driving him to despair of self-righteousness, and so to Christ. They mark Calvin’s point that the law slays its own disciples: it does not need Christ to abolish its righteousness, for it pronounces death on all who seek life by it. They observe the liberating purpose: “that I might live unto God” — the death to the law is not the end but the means; freed from the law as a system of justification and condemnation, the believer now lives a new life directed wholly to God. They note Wesley’s sense that this death is to all hope of justification from the law, opening the way to true life. The verse thus answers the charge of verse 17 fully: dying to the law is not lawlessness but the path to living to God; the law’s own work ends in the believer’s death to it and his new life in Christ — a life Paul will now describe as the indwelling of Christ himself.
20:
On Galatians 2:20 — “I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me: and the life which I now live in the flesh I live by the faith of the Son of God, who loved me, and gave himself for me” — the witnesses mark the believer’s union with Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the mystical depth. Robertson: “I have been crucified with Christ” (perfect passive, synestauromai) — “one of Paul’s greatest mystical sayings” (Romans 6:6). The Puritan Henry: the spiritual or hidden life of a believer — “sin is mortified, and grace is quickened.” The Lutheran Luther: quickened by Christ’s death, “I now live indeed.” Wesley: “I, as to my corrupt nature, live no longer.”

The verse soars into the believer’s union with Christ. Paul is crucified with Christ; yet he lives — no longer he, but Christ in him; and his present life he lives by faith in the Son of God, who loved him and gave himself for him.

So the witnesses see Paul unfold the new life that death to the law opens: the indwelling of Christ. The witnesses note the foundation: “I am crucified with Christ” — united to Christ in his death, the old self with its bondage to law and sin put to death on the cross. They mark the paradox of the new life: “nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me” — the believer truly lives, yet the source and power of that life is no longer his own self but Christ dwelling within him. They observe the manner of this life: “I live by the faith of the Son of God” — the present life in the body is sustained moment by moment by trust in Christ. They note the tender, personal ground that crowns it: “who loved me, and gave himself for me” — Paul makes the universal atonement his own, the love of Christ individualized to the single soul. They observe Henry’s point that this is the hidden, spiritual life of grace. The verse thus answers fully the charge that grace breeds sin: the justified believer is not lawless but crucified with Christ and indwelt by him, living by faith in the Son who loved him — the new life of union that the law could never give.
21:
On Galatians 2:21 — “I do not frustrate the grace of God: for if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain” — the witnesses mark the conclusion against the law.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force. Robertson: “I do not make void the grace of God” (atheto, make ineffective). Adam Clarke: to preach the law’s necessity is to “contemn… the grace of God.” The Reformed Calvin: to despise grace, obtained at such a price, is dreadful ingratitude. The Lutheran Luther: to seek justification by works is to reject the grace of God.

The verse drives the argument to its sharp conclusion. Paul does not nullify God’s grace; for if righteousness could come by the law, then Christ died for nothing. The cross itself proves the law cannot justify.

So the witnesses see Paul clinch the whole argument with an unanswerable conclusion. The witnesses note the disclaimer: “I do not frustrate the grace of God” — I do not make it void, ineffective, set it aside; the charge belongs not to Paul but to the Judaizers, who by adding the law nullify grace. They mark the decisive reasoning: “if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain” — if a man could be justified by keeping the law, there was no need for Christ to die; his death would be pointless, a wasted sacrifice. They observe that the cross is therefore the standing proof that the law cannot justify: God would not have given his Son to die had the law sufficed. They note Luther’s and Calvin’s sense of the dreadful ingratitude of seeking justification by works, which despises the grace bought at so great a price and empties the cross of meaning. The verse thus seals the doctrine and the chapter: to add the law to faith for justification is to make grace void and Christ’s death needless. Grace and law, as grounds of righteousness, are mutually exclusive — and the death of Christ forever settles the matter on the side of grace.
